articles.headline: Supreme Court Declares 'Right to Clean Air' as Fundamental Right under Article 21
articles.explainer:
• In a landmark judgment, the Supreme Court of India has ruled that the 'Right to Clean Air' is an intrinsic part of the Right to Life and Personal Liberty (Article 21).
• The ruling mandates central and state governments to take more proactive and stringent measures to combat air pollution.
• Emphasizes the state's responsibility to protect environmental health for present and future generations.
• The judgment stems from a Public Interest Litigation (PIL) highlighting the severe health impacts of worsening air quality across major Indian cities.
prelimsPoints: Article 21 of the Indian Constitution: Right to Life and Personal Liberty.
Judicial expansion of Article 21 to include various environmental rights.
Concept of Public Interest Litigation (PIL) for environmental protection.
Role of the Supreme Court in environmental jurisprudence.
mainsPoints: Discuss the implications of declaring 'Right to Clean Air' as a fundamental right on government policy and industrial practices.
Analyze the scope and limits of judicial activism in environmental governance in India.
Examine the challenges in implementing such a fundamental right, considering socio-economic factors and development imperatives.
Evaluate the 'Polluter Pays Principle' and 'Public Trust Doctrine' in the context of this judgment.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel placed on a law book with a backdrop of clear blue sky and a city skyline, symbolizing environmental justice.
articles.trivia: The Supreme Court of India has previously expanded Article 21 to include rights like the right to live with human dignity, right to livelihood, right to privacy, and right to clean environment.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Indian Polity and Governance
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Right to Life and Personal Liberty; Environmental jurisprudence

articles.headline: Parliament Passes National Urban Health Mission (NUHM) 2.0 Bill
articles.explainer:
• The Parliament has successfully passed the National Urban Health Mission (NUHM) 2.0 Bill, aiming to strengthen urban healthcare infrastructure.
• Focuses on improving primary healthcare services, especially for vulnerable populations in urban areas.
• Introduces provisions for digital health records, telemedicine, and community health workers in urban settings.
• The mission seeks to address the growing health disparities and infrastructure gaps in India's rapidly urbanizing landscape.
prelimsPoints: National Urban Health Mission (NUHM) is a sub-mission of the National Health Mission (NHM).
NHM comprises two sub-missions: National Rural Health Mission (NRHM) and NUHM.
Key objectives include reducing out-of-pocket health expenditure and improving access to quality healthcare.
mainsPoints: Analyze the challenges faced by urban healthcare systems in India and how NUHM 2.0 seeks to address them.
Discuss the role of digital health and telemedicine in achieving universal health coverage in urban areas.
Evaluate the effectiveness of community health worker models in improving urban health outcomes.
Examine the integration of NUHM 2.0 with other urban development schemes and its potential impact on smart cities.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting urban healthcare workers interacting with residents, with digital health symbols.
articles.trivia: India's urban population is projected to reach 600 million by 2030, necessitating robust urban health infrastructure.

articles.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Global Economic Uncertainties
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) has decided to keep the repo rate unchanged at its latest meeting.
• The decision comes amidst persistent global economic uncertainties and a cautious approach to domestic inflation management.
• The RBI reiterated its commitment to supporting economic growth while ensuring price stability.
• Analysts suggest the move reflects a wait-and-watch approach as global central banks navigate inflationary pressures and potential recession risks.
prelimsPoints: Repo Rate: Rate at which commercial banks borrow money from the RBI.
Monetary Policy Committee (MPC): Statutory body responsible for fixing the benchmark interest rate.
Primary objective of monetary policy in India: maintaining price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Quantitative and qualitative tools of monetary policy.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors influencing the RBI's decision to maintain the status quo on the repo rate.
Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control with economic growth objectives.
Examine the impact of global economic uncertainties on India's monetary policy decisions.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current monetary policy tools in managing India's economic landscape.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting an interest rate graph with a steady line, overlaid with RBI logo.
articles.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) was constituted in 2016 and consists of six members – three officials of the RBI and three external experts nominated by the Government of India.

articles.headline: India-ASEAN FTA Review Concludes, New Protocols to Boost Trade and Investment
articles.explainer:
• The comprehensive review of the India-ASEAN Free Trade Agreement (FTA) has successfully concluded, paving the way for new protocols.
• The updated agreement aims to address existing trade imbalances and enhance market access for Indian goods and services.
• Focuses on streamlining customs procedures, promoting digital trade, and fostering greater investment flows between India and ASEAN nations.
• This development is expected to strengthen India's 'Act East' policy and regional economic integration.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, Regional and Global Groupings and Agreements
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
prelimsPoints: ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) members and its significance.
India's 'Act East' Policy and its evolution from 'Look East'.
Key provisions of a Free Trade Agreement (FTA).
Major trade partners of India in Southeast Asia.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the updated India-ASEAN FTA for India's economic growth and strategic interests in Southeast Asia.
Discuss the potential impact of new protocols on specific sectors of the Indian economy and its trade balance with ASEAN.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India in strengthening its economic ties with the ASEAN bloc.
Examine how the revised FTA aligns with India's broader foreign policy objectives and regional security architecture.
articles.imageDescription: A map highlighting India and ASEAN countries connected by trade lines.
articles.trivia: ASEAN was established on August 8, 1967, in Bangkok, Thailand, with the signing of the ASEAN Declaration (Bangkok Declaration) by the founding fathers of ASEAN.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Tests Next-Gen Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Prototype
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) has achieved a significant milestone with the successful test flight of its next-generation Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) prototype.
• The test involved a controlled landing experiment, simulating the return of a spacecraft to Earth.
• This technology is crucial for reducing the cost of space launches and enhancing India's competitive edge in the global space market.
• The RLV program aims to develop fully reusable orbital launch vehicles, similar to space shuttle technology.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in Science & Technology
prelimsPoints: RLV-TD (Reusable Launch Vehicle – Technology Demonstrator) is a precursor to a two-stage-to-orbit (TSTO) fully reusable launch vehicle.
Objectives of RLV program: reduce launch costs, increase launch frequency.
Other countries/agencies developing RLV technology (e.g., SpaceX, Blue Origin).
Different stages of ISRO's RLV development.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of Reusable Launch Vehicle technology for India's space program and its economic implications.
Analyze the challenges and technological advancements required for developing fully reusable orbital launch vehicles.
Evaluate how RLV technology can boost India's position in the global commercial space launch market.
Examine the potential applications of RLV beyond satellite launches, such as space tourism or in-orbit servicing.
articles.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of ISRO's RLV prototype descending towards a landing strip.
articles.trivia: ISRO first successfully tested the RLV-TD in 2016, demonstrating its hypersonic flight and re-entry capabilities.

articles.headline: Government Unveils New 'Blue Economy' Policy Framework for Sustainable Ocean Resources
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government has announced a comprehensive 'Blue Economy' policy framework aimed at sustainable utilization of India's ocean resources.
• The policy focuses on marine fisheries, aquaculture, ocean energy, marine biotechnology, and coastal tourism.
• Emphasizes job creation, environmental conservation, and enhancing India's maritime security and trade.
• It seeks to integrate various ocean-related sectors to unlock the economic potential of India's vast coastline and Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ).
prelimsPoints: Concept of 'Blue Economy' and its pillars.
India's coastline length and Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ).
Key initiatives under the 'Deep Ocean Mission'.
Importance of oceans for India's economy and security.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of India's 'Blue Economy' and the challenges in its sustainable development.
Analyze how the new policy framework can contribute to India's economic growth and environmental conservation goals.
Examine the role of technology and international cooperation in harnessing ocean resources sustainably.
Evaluate the interlinkages between the Blue Economy, coastal community livelihoods, and maritime security.
articles.imageDescription: An infographic showing various components of the Blue Economy like shipping, fisheries, and renewable energy.
articles.trivia: India's coastline is over 7,500 km long, with an Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) of over 2 million sq km, rich in living and non-living resources.

articles.headline: S. Krishnaswamy, Veteran Documentarist and Cultural Chronicler, Passes Away at 87
articles.explainer:
• Renowned Indian documentarian and film historian, S. Krishnaswamy, has passed away at the age of 87 due to heart disease.
• Krishnaswamy was celebrated for his extensive work documenting India's cultural heritage, arts, and social issues over several decades.
• His films and writings provided invaluable insights into various facets of Indian life and history.
• He was also known for his efforts in preserving film archives and promoting film education in the country.
prelimsPoints: Notable Indian documentarians and their contributions.
Significance of documentary filmmaking in chronicling history and culture.
Institutions involved in film preservation in India (e.g., National Film Archive of India).
mainsPoints: Discuss the role of documentary films in shaping public discourse and preserving cultural memory in India.
Analyze the contributions of individuals like S. Krishnaswamy to the fields of Indian cinema and cultural studies.
Examine the challenges faced by independent documentarians and film archivists in India.
Evaluate the importance of documenting diverse aspects of Indian society for future generations.
articles.imageDescription: A black and white photograph of S. Krishnaswamy, possibly holding a film reel or camera.
articles.trivia: Krishnaswamy's work often explored themes of national integration, religious harmony, and the struggles of ordinary people.

articles.headline: Divine Fusion: Kuchipudi Dancer and Pichwai Artist Amalgamate Traditional Art Forms
articles.explainer:
• A unique collaborative project titled 'Divine Fusion' has brought together a Kuchipudi dancer and a Pichwai artist.
• The initiative aims to explore the synergistic possibilities between the classical Indian dance form and the traditional Rajasthani painting style.
• The performance integrates live Pichwai painting with Kuchipudi choreography, offering a multi-sensory experience.
• This amalgamation enriches the understanding of both art forms, highlighting their shared narratives and aesthetic principles.
prelimsPoints: Origin and characteristics of Kuchipudi dance form (Andhra Pradesh).
Origin and characteristics of Pichwai painting (Nathdwara, Rajasthan), typically depicting Lord Krishna.
Other classical Indian dance forms and their regional origins.
Examples of fusion in Indian art and culture.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of interdisciplinary collaborations in preserving and evolving traditional Indian art forms.
Analyze how the 'Divine Fusion' project contributes to a deeper understanding and appreciation of Kuchipudi and Pichwai.
Examine the role of artists in reinterpreting classical traditions for contemporary audiences.
Evaluate the potential of such fusions to promote cultural tourism and economic opportunities for artists.
articles.imageDescription: A Kuchipudi dancer performing gracefully while a Pichwai artist paints in the background, showing their collaborative synergy.
articles.trivia: Pichwai paintings often depict scenes from Krishna's life, particularly those related to the Shrinathji manifestation at Nathdwara, Rajasthan.

articles.headline: A Zeitgeist of Bookmaking: New Research on Medieval Desh Bhasha Manuscripts and Hindi Evolution
articles.explainer:
• Tyler Williams' literary historiography offers an engaging exploration of the materiality of medieval desh bhasha manuscripts.
• The research sheds light on the evolution of Hindi language and its literary traditions through the study of ancient texts.
• It delves into the processes of bookmaking, scribal practices, and the cultural context of manuscript production in medieval India.
• The study provides fresh perspectives on how regional languages contributed to the linguistic and literary landscape of the subcontinent.
prelimsPoints: Concept of 'Desh Bhasha' in medieval Indian linguistic context.
Evolution of Hindi from Apabhramsha and other regional dialects.
Important literary works and authors of medieval Hindi literature (e.g., Bhakti and Sufi poets).
Methods of manuscript preservation and study.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of studying medieval manuscripts for understanding the evolution of Indian languages and literature.
Analyze the role of 'desh bhasha' literature in shaping the cultural and social fabric of medieval India.
Examine the challenges in preserving and digitizing ancient manuscripts for future generations.
Evaluate how such research contributes to a more nuanced understanding of India's linguistic diversity and heritage.
articles.imageDescription: An ancient, ornate Indian manuscript with intricate calligraphy and illustrations.
articles.trivia: The term 'desh bhasha' generally refers to regional languages that evolved from Prakrit and Apabhramsha, distinct from Sanskrit, and were used by common people.

articles.headline: New Book Investigates Militant Buddhism and Its Transformation in Asia
articles.explainer:
• A recently published book offers an in-depth investigation into the rise and impact of militant Buddhism across various parts of Asia.
• The author explores the historical roots and contemporary manifestations of nationalist and extremist elements within Buddhist communities.
• The study analyzes how political and social factors have led to the transformation of Buddhism from a pacifist religion to one sometimes associated with violence.
• It sheds light on complex geopolitical dynamics and inter-religious relations in several Asian nations.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, Regional and Global Groupings and Agreements
prelimsPoints: Basic tenets of Buddhism, including Ahimsa (non-violence).
Historical spread of Buddhism in Asia.
Examples of religious nationalism in different parts of the world.
Countries where Buddhist nationalism has been observed (e.g., Myanmar, Sri Lanka).
mainsPoints: Discuss the factors contributing to the rise of militant religious movements, specifically focusing on Buddhist nationalism in Asia.
Analyze the implications of such movements for regional stability and inter-religious harmony.
Examine how political and socio-economic grievances can be exploited to fuel religious extremism.
Evaluate the role of state and non-state actors in either mitigating or exacerbating religious conflicts in Asia.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized image depicting contrasting symbols of peace (lotus) and conflict (fist) to represent the theme.
articles.trivia: Buddhism originated in ancient India with Siddhartha Gautama (Buddha) and emphasizes the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path to achieve Nirvana.

articles.headline: Beautiful and Broken: Insider's View Critiques Commercialisation of Art World
articles.explainer:
• A new book offers an insider's perspective on the contemporary art world, critiquing its increasing commercialization.
• The author provides a rollicking read while offering serious commentary on how market forces influence artistic creation and appreciation.
• It delves into the dynamics of art galleries, auction houses, and the commodification of art.
• The book raises important questions about artistic integrity, accessibility, and the true value of art beyond its monetary price.
gsSubTopics: Globalization and its effects on Indian Society
mainsPoints: Discuss the impact of commercialization on various art forms in India and globally.
Analyze the ethical dilemmas faced by artists and institutions in a market-driven art world.
Examine the role of art in society beyond its economic value and how commercialization might undermine it.
Evaluate strategies to promote artistic expression and appreciation while navigating market realities.
articles.imageDescription: A broken piece of classical sculpture intertwined with modern currency notes, symbolizing the theme.
articles.trivia: The global art market was valued at approximately $67.8 billion in 2022, with a significant portion driven by high-net-worth collectors.

articles.headline: New Literary Work Explores Themes of Loss and 'Invisible Residue' in Kerala Home
articles.explainer:
• A poignant new literary piece delves into the aftermath of a mother's sudden death, leaving two daughters as 'invisible residue' in a vast Kerala home.
• The narrative explores themes of grief, memory, family dynamics, and the psychological impact of loss on individuals.
• It offers a nuanced portrayal of domestic life and the silent struggles within traditional Indian households.
• The work highlights the emotional complexities and unspoken narratives that often define personal spaces and relationships.
mainsPoints: Discuss how contemporary Indian literature reflects and critiques societal norms and family structures, particularly in the context of women's experiences.
Analyze the portrayal of grief and loss in literature as a tool for social commentary.
Examine the cultural nuances of family dynamics in specific regions of India, such as Kerala, as depicted in literary works.
Evaluate the power of narrative in bringing to light 'invisible' social or emotional issues within private spheres.
articles.imageDescription: An artistic depiction of an empty, traditional Kerala home, evoking a sense of loss and memory.
articles.trivia: Kerala's traditional architecture, often characterized by 'Nalukettu' (four-block homes), reflects distinct cultural and social structures.

articles.headline: Government Launches 'PM-Gati Shakti for Urban Infrastructure' Initiative
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government has announced a new initiative, 'PM-Gati Shakti for Urban Infrastructure', extending the national master plan to cities.
• Aims to integrate and streamline planning and implementation of urban infrastructure projects, including roads, public transport, and utilities.
• Utilizes geospatial technology and digital platforms for efficient resource allocation and project monitoring.
• The initiative seeks to improve urban connectivity, reduce logistics costs, and enhance the overall quality of life in Indian cities.
articles.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan: its original scope and objectives.
Role of geospatial technology in infrastructure planning.
Challenges of urban infrastructure development in India.
Government initiatives for urban development (e.g., Smart Cities Mission, AMRUT).
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of extending the PM Gati Shakti framework to urban infrastructure development.
Analyze how integrated planning and digital tools can address the complexities of urban logistics and connectivity.
Examine the potential impact of this initiative on urban economic growth, environmental sustainability, and quality of life.
Evaluate the challenges in inter-agency coordination and land acquisition for urban infrastructure projects under the new framework.
articles.imageDescription: An aerial view of a smart city network with interconnected infrastructure, overlaid with digital data streams.
articles.trivia: The original PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan was launched in October 2021 to break departmental silos and bring more holistic and integrated planning and execution of infrastructure projects.

articles.headline: India and Japan Conclude Joint Naval Exercise in Indian Ocean, Bolstering Maritime Security
articles.explainer:
• India and Japan have successfully concluded a major joint naval exercise in the Indian Ocean region.
• The exercise focused on enhancing interoperability, maritime surveillance, and anti-submarine warfare capabilities.
• It underscores the growing strategic partnership between the two nations and their shared commitment to a free and open Indo-Pacific.
• Such drills are crucial for addressing emerging maritime challenges and ensuring regional stability amidst evolving geopolitical dynamics.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, Regional and Global Groupings and Agreements
Security Challenges and their Management in Border Areas
prelimsPoints: Major joint military exercises between India and Japan (e.g., JIMEX, Dharma Guardian).
Significance of the Indian Ocean Region (IOR) for global trade and security.
Concept of 'free and open Indo-Pacific' and its proponents.
Role of Quad grouping (India, US, Japan, Australia) in regional security.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic rationale behind increasing India-Japan defence cooperation, particularly in the maritime domain.
Analyze how joint naval exercises contribute to maritime security and stability in the Indo-Pacific region.
Examine the challenges and opportunities for India in leveraging partnerships to counter emerging security threats in the Indian Ocean.
Evaluate the role of such bilateral exercises in strengthening India's 'Act East' policy and broader geopolitical objectives.
articles.imageDescription: Two warships, one Indian and one Japanese, sailing in formation during a naval exercise.
articles.trivia: India and Japan have been conducting joint naval exercises, known as 'JIMEX' (Japan-India Maritime Exercise), since 2012.

articles.headline: AI-Powered Diagnostics See Wider Adoption in India's Public Health System
articles.explainer:
• Artificial Intelligence (AI) powered diagnostic tools are being increasingly integrated into India's public health system, particularly in remote and underserved areas.
• These tools aid in early and accurate detection of diseases like tuberculosis, diabetes, and various cancers, improving patient outcomes.
• The government is promoting pilot projects and partnerships to scale up AI-enabled healthcare solutions across primary health centers.
• This development aims to bridge the gap in specialist availability and enhance diagnostic capabilities, especially in rural India.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- Developments and their Applications
prelimsPoints: Applications of AI in various sectors, including healthcare.
Government initiatives for digital health in India (e.g., Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission).
Challenges in healthcare access and diagnostics in rural India.
Examples of AI diagnostic tools (e.g., for radiology, pathology).
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of AI-powered diagnostics in improving public health outcomes in India.
Analyze the ethical considerations and challenges associated with the widespread adoption of AI in healthcare, particularly concerning data privacy and algorithmic bias.
Examine how AI can address the shortage of medical specialists and enhance healthcare accessibility in remote areas.
Evaluate the government's role in creating an enabling ecosystem for AI innovation and deployment in the health sector.
articles.imageDescription: A doctor using a tablet with AI-powered diagnostic software, showing a medical scan.
articles.trivia: India is one of the fastest-growing markets for AI in healthcare, with significant investments in areas like drug discovery, personalized medicine, and diagnostics.

articles.headline: Himalayan States Collaborate on Sustainable Tourism Framework to Protect Fragile Ecosystems
articles.explainer:
• Several Indian Himalayan states have joined forces to develop a common framework for sustainable tourism.
• The initiative aims to regulate tourist activities, promote eco-friendly practices, and protect the fragile Himalayan ecosystems from over-tourism.
• Focuses on community-based tourism, waste management, and responsible infrastructure development.
• This collaborative effort seeks to balance economic benefits from tourism with the imperative of environmental conservation in the sensitive mountain regions.
prelimsPoints: Key Himalayan states in India and their ecological significance.
Impact of tourism on fragile mountain ecosystems.
Concept of 'carrying capacity' in tourism.
Examples of eco-tourism initiatives in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges faced by the Himalayan states due to over-tourism and climate change.
Analyze how a collaborative sustainable tourism framework can mitigate environmental degradation and promote responsible tourism.
Examine the role of local communities in developing and managing sustainable tourism practices in mountain regions.
Evaluate the balance between economic development through tourism and the need for ecological preservation in sensitive areas.
articles.imageDescription: A serene landscape of the Himalayas with a small, eco-friendly homestay, symbolizing sustainable tourism.
articles.trivia: The Himalayas are home to a vast array of biodiversity, including many endemic species, and are often referred to as the 'Water Tower of Asia' due to their massive ice and snow reserves.

articles.headline: Lok Sabha Passes Data Protection Amendment Bill, 2025
articles.explainer:
• The Lok Sabha has passed the Data Protection Amendment Bill, 2025, introducing significant changes to India's data privacy landscape.
• The bill aims to update the existing data protection framework to align with global standards and address emerging digital challenges.
• Key provisions include stricter consent requirements, enhanced data fiduciary obligations, and a framework for cross-border data transfers.
• It seeks to balance individual privacy rights with national security and economic interests, following extensive stakeholder consultations.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- Developments and their Applications
prelimsPoints: Previous attempts at data protection legislation in India (e.g., Justice Srikrishna Committee report).
Key principles of data protection (e.g., purpose limitation, data minimization).
Global data protection regulations (e.g., GDPR).
Difference between personal data and sensitive personal data.
mainsPoints: Discuss the salient features and potential impact of the Data Protection Amendment Bill, 2025 on individual privacy and digital economy in India.
Analyze the challenges in implementing a comprehensive data protection framework in a digitally diverse country like India.
Examine how the bill balances fundamental rights to privacy with national security and legitimate state interests.
Evaluate the role of the proposed Data Protection Board and its efficacy in enforcing the new regulations.
articles.imageDescription: A digital padlock icon overlaid on a map of India, symbolizing data security and privacy.
articles.trivia: India's first attempt at a comprehensive data protection law came after the Supreme Court's landmark Puttaswamy judgment in 2017, which declared privacy as a fundamental right.

articles.headline: India's Q3 FY25 GDP Growth Forecast Revised Upwards by Leading Agencies
articles.explainer:
• Several prominent economic agencies have revised India's GDP growth forecast for the third quarter of Fiscal Year 2025 upwards.
• The optimistic outlook is attributed to strong domestic demand, robust manufacturing output, and a resurgence in private investment.
• This positive revision signals resilience in the Indian economy amidst a challenging global economic environment.
• The government's continued focus on capital expenditure and structural reforms is seen as a key driver for sustained growth momentum.
prelimsPoints: Definition of GDP and its components (consumption, investment, government spending, net exports).
Methods of calculating GDP (expenditure method, income method, production method).
Role of different sectors (agriculture, industry, services) in India's GDP.
Major economic indicators tracked by government and agencies.
mainsPoints: Discuss the key drivers contributing to India's upward revised GDP growth forecast for Q3 FY25.
Analyze the significance of strong domestic demand and private investment for sustainable economic growth in India.
Examine the challenges that could potentially hinder India's long-term growth trajectory despite short-term positives.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the government's fiscal policies and structural reforms in boosting economic resilience.
articles.imageDescription: An upward trending graph with Indian Rupee symbols and a growth arrow.
articles.trivia: India is projected to be one of the fastest-growing major economies globally in the coming years, driven by its large domestic market and demographic dividend.

articles.headline: Diverse New Books Hit the Shelves, Covering History, Culture, and Social Commentary
articles.explainer:
• A fresh wave of books has been released, offering a wide array of genres from historical analyses to contemporary fiction.
• The collection includes works exploring cultural transformations, deep dives into historical literature, and critiques of modern societal trends.
• Readers can find everything from engaging literary historiography to poignant narratives about family and loss.
• This season's releases promise intellectual stimulation and diverse perspectives on India and the world.
articles.imageDescription: A stack of diverse new books on a bookshelf, with varied titles and covers.
articles.trivia: India is one of the world's largest publishers of books, with a significant output in English and various regional languages.

articles.headline: Critique: Robert Galbraith's Latest Crime Thriller Fails to Impress, Dubbed 'Trek to Nowhere'
articles.explainer:
• Robert Galbraith, also known as J.K. Rowling, has released a new crime thriller that has received a lukewarm critical reception.
• Reviewers have labeled the book a 'criminal waste of time and energy,' citing its excessive length and unengaging plot.
• The critique highlights issues with pacing, character development, and narrative structure, which detract from the reading experience.
• This assessment suggests that even established authors can sometimes miss the mark with new genre entries.
articles.imageDescription: A blurred image of a book cover with a magnifying glass, symbolizing a crime thriller and critical review.
articles.trivia: J.K. Rowling adopted the pseudonym Robert Galbraith to publish her crime novels featuring private detective Cormoran Strike, initially to publish without hype.

articles.headline: New Murder Mystery Explores Themes of Wronged Women and Social Justice
articles.explainer:
• A new murder mystery novel delves into a complex plot centered around wronged women and their pursuit of justice.
• The narrative combines elements of suspense and social commentary, highlighting systemic injustices faced by women.
• It explores themes of retribution, empowerment, and the often-overlooked struggles of marginalized individuals.
• This book is part of a growing trend in literature that uses popular genres to address serious social issues.
mainsPoints: Discuss how contemporary crime fiction can serve as a powerful medium for social commentary on issues like gender inequality and justice for marginalized groups.
Analyze the evolving portrayal of women in Indian literature, from victims to agents of change.
Examine the role of popular genres in raising awareness about complex social issues and influencing public perception.
Evaluate the effectiveness of narrative in highlighting systemic injustices and advocating for social reforms.
articles.imageDescription: A silhouette of a woman standing in front of a mysterious, dark backdrop, symbolizing justice and mystery.
articles.trivia: Many historical and mythological narratives in India feature strong female characters seeking justice against societal wrongs, predating modern feminist literature.

highlights: Supreme Court declares 'Right to Clean Air' a fundamental right under Article 21, mandating stricter pollution controls.
Parliament passes NUHM 2.0 Bill to enhance urban healthcare, focusing on primary services and digital health.
ISRO successfully tests next-gen Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) prototype, aiming to cut space launch costs.
Government unveils comprehensive 'Blue Economy' policy framework for sustainable utilization of India's ocean resources.
India-ASEAN FTA review concludes with new protocols to boost trade, strengthening 'Act East' policy.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the 'Right to Clean Air' recently declared as a fundamental right by the Supreme Court, consider the following statements:
1. It has been interpreted as part of the Right to Life and Personal Liberty under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution.
2. The judgment mandates only the Central Government to implement stricter air pollution control measures.
3. It emphasizes the principle of inter-generational equity in environmental protection.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The Supreme Court has consistently expanded the ambit of Article 21 to include various environmental rights, including the right to clean air. Statement 2 is incorrect: The judgment mandates both central and state governments to implement stricter measures. Statement 3 is correct: Inter-generational equity is a key principle in environmental jurisprudence, ensuring that current generations meet their needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following statements best describes the primary objective of the National Urban Health Mission (NUHM)?
options: (a) To provide universal health insurance coverage to all urban residents.
(b) To strengthen primary healthcare services for urban poor and vulnerable populations.
(c) To establish super-specialty hospitals in all Tier-1 and Tier-2 cities.
(d) To focus solely on sanitation and hygiene in urban slums.
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) To strengthen primary healthcare services for urban poor and vulnerable populations.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The National Urban Health Mission (NUHM) is a sub-mission of the National Health Mission (NHM) and primarily aims to meet the health needs of the urban poor and other vulnerable groups, by making available to them essential primary healthcare services and reducing their out-of-pocket health expenditure.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) of the Reserve Bank of India:
1. The MPC is a statutory body responsible for fixing the benchmark interest rate (repo rate).
2. It consists of seven members, four from the RBI and three external experts.
3. The primary objective of the MPC is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct: The MPC is a statutory body constituted under the RBI Act, 1934, for fixing the benchmark interest rate. Statement 2 is incorrect: The MPC consists of six members – three officials of the RBI and three external experts nominated by the Government of India. Statement 3 is correct: As per the RBI Act, the primary objective of monetary policy is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to India's 'Blue Economy' policy framework, which of the following sectors are explicitly emphasized?
1. Marine fisheries and aquaculture
2. Ocean energy and marine biotechnology
3. Coastal tourism and maritime security

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: The 'Blue Economy' policy framework typically encompasses a wide range of ocean-related economic activities. The article explicitly mentions marine fisheries, aquaculture, ocean energy, marine biotechnology, coastal tourism, and enhancing maritime security and trade, covering all three options.

prelimsMocks.question: What is the primary goal of ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) program?
options: (a) To develop a manned mission to Mars by 2030.
(b) To reduce the cost of space launches and enhance India's competitive edge.
(c) To establish a permanent Indian space station in low Earth orbit.
(d) To develop advanced satellite communication systems for military use.
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) To reduce the cost of space launches and enhance India's competitive edge.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The primary goal of ISRO's RLV program, like similar initiatives globally, is to significantly reduce the cost of accessing space by making launch vehicles reusable, thereby enhancing competitiveness in the commercial space market.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's declaration of 'Right to Clean Air' as a fundamental right underscores the evolving nature of environmental jurisprudence in India. Discuss the implications of this judgment for environmental governance and sustainable development in the country.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors; GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the judgment and its basis in Article 21. Discuss implications for government responsibility, industrial compliance, and citizen's recourse. Analyze challenges in implementation, balancing development and environment. Conclude with the broader impact on India's sustainable development goals and judicial activism.

mainsMocks.question: India's 'Act East' policy has gained significant momentum through economic and strategic partnerships. In this context, analyze the importance of the recently concluded India-ASEAN FTA review for enhancing India's economic integration and maritime security in the Indo-Pacific region.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests; GS3: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
mainsMocks.approach: Briefly explain 'Act East' policy and India-ASEAN relations. Discuss the significance of the FTA review in boosting trade, investment, and market access. Link economic integration with strategic objectives like maritime security in the Indo-Pacific. Address potential challenges and opportunities for India.

mainsMocks.question: Artificial Intelligence (AI) holds immense potential to revolutionize India's public health system, particularly in diagnostics. Critically examine the opportunities and ethical challenges associated with the widespread adoption of AI-powered diagnostic tools in Indian healthcare.
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life; GS2: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce AI in healthcare and its diagnostic potential. Discuss opportunities like early detection, addressing specialist shortages, and improving access in rural areas. Analyze ethical challenges such as data privacy, algorithmic bias, accountability, and the digital divide. Conclude with policy recommendations for responsible AI integration.

quickBytes.text: S. Krishnaswamy, a veteran documentarian and film historian, passed away at 87.
Kuchipudi, a classical Indian dance, originates from Andhra Pradesh.
Pichwai paintings, depicting Lord Krishna, hail from Nathdwara, Rajasthan.
The 'Polluter Pays Principle' holds polluters accountable for environmental damage.
The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) has six members, three from RBI and three external.
India's 'Act East' Policy is an upgrade of the earlier 'Look East' Policy.
RLV-TD is ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle – Technology Demonstrator program.
India has a coastline of over 7,500 km and an EEZ of over 2 million sq km.
PM Gati Shakti is a national master plan for multimodal connectivity infrastructure.
The Puttaswamy judgment (2017) declared privacy as a fundamental right in India.

GS1.headline: S. Krishnaswamy, Veteran Documentarist and Cultural Chronicler, Passes Away at 87
GS1.explainer:
• Renowned Indian documentarian and film historian, S. Krishnaswamy, has passed away at the age of 87 due to heart disease.
• Krishnaswamy was celebrated for his extensive work documenting India's cultural heritage, arts, and social issues over several decades.
• His films and writings provided invaluable insights into various facets of Indian life and history.
• He was also known for his efforts in preserving film archives and promoting film education in the country.
prelimsPoints: Notable Indian documentarians and their contributions.
Significance of documentary filmmaking in chronicling history and culture.
Institutions involved in film preservation in India (e.g., National Film Archive of India).
mainsPoints: Discuss the role of documentary films in shaping public discourse and preserving cultural memory in India.
Analyze the contributions of individuals like S. Krishnaswamy to the fields of Indian cinema and cultural studies.
Examine the challenges faced by independent documentarians and film archivists in India.
Evaluate the importance of documenting diverse aspects of Indian society for future generations.
GS1.imageDescription: A black and white photograph of S. Krishnaswamy, possibly holding a film reel or camera.
GS1.trivia: Krishnaswamy's work often explored themes of national integration, religious harmony, and the struggles of ordinary people.

GS1.headline: Divine Fusion: Kuchipudi Dancer and Pichwai Artist Amalgamate Traditional Art Forms
GS1.explainer:
• A unique collaborative project titled 'Divine Fusion' has brought together a Kuchipudi dancer and a Pichwai artist.
• The initiative aims to explore the synergistic possibilities between the classical Indian dance form and the traditional Rajasthani painting style.
• The performance integrates live Pichwai painting with Kuchipudi choreography, offering a multi-sensory experience.
• This amalgamation enriches the understanding of both art forms, highlighting their shared narratives and aesthetic principles.
prelimsPoints: Origin and characteristics of Kuchipudi dance form (Andhra Pradesh).
Origin and characteristics of Pichwai painting (Nathdwara, Rajasthan), typically depicting Lord Krishna.
Other classical Indian dance forms and their regional origins.
Examples of fusion in Indian art and culture.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of interdisciplinary collaborations in preserving and evolving traditional Indian art forms.
Analyze how the 'Divine Fusion' project contributes to a deeper understanding and appreciation of Kuchipudi and Pichwai.
Examine the role of artists in reinterpreting classical traditions for contemporary audiences.
Evaluate the potential of such fusions to promote cultural tourism and economic opportunities for artists.
GS1.imageDescription: A Kuchipudi dancer performing gracefully while a Pichwai artist paints in the background, showing their collaborative synergy.
GS1.trivia: Pichwai paintings often depict scenes from Krishna's life, particularly those related to the Shrinathji manifestation at Nathdwara, Rajasthan.

GS1.headline: A Zeitgeist of Bookmaking: New Research on Medieval Desh Bhasha Manuscripts and Hindi Evolution
GS1.explainer:
• Tyler Williams' literary historiography offers an engaging exploration of the materiality of medieval desh bhasha manuscripts.
• The research sheds light on the evolution of Hindi language and its literary traditions through the study of ancient texts.
• It delves into the processes of bookmaking, scribal practices, and the cultural context of manuscript production in medieval India.
• The study provides fresh perspectives on how regional languages contributed to the linguistic and literary landscape of the subcontinent.
prelimsPoints: Concept of 'Desh Bhasha' in medieval Indian linguistic context.
Evolution of Hindi from Apabhramsha and other regional dialects.
Important literary works and authors of medieval Hindi literature (e.g., Bhakti and Sufi poets).
Methods of manuscript preservation and study.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of studying medieval manuscripts for understanding the evolution of Indian languages and literature.
Analyze the role of 'desh bhasha' literature in shaping the cultural and social fabric of medieval India.
Examine the challenges in preserving and digitizing ancient manuscripts for future generations.
Evaluate how such research contributes to a more nuanced understanding of India's linguistic diversity and heritage.
GS1.imageDescription: An ancient, ornate Indian manuscript with intricate calligraphy and illustrations.
GS1.trivia: The term 'desh bhasha' generally refers to regional languages that evolved from Prakrit and Apabhramsha, distinct from Sanskrit, and were used by common people.

GS1.headline: New Literary Work Explores Themes of Loss and 'Invisible Residue' in Kerala Home
GS1.explainer:
• A poignant new literary piece delves into the aftermath of a mother's sudden death, leaving two daughters as 'invisible residue' in a vast Kerala home.
• The narrative explores themes of grief, memory, family dynamics, and the psychological impact of loss on individuals.
• It offers a nuanced portrayal of domestic life and the silent struggles within traditional Indian households.
• The work highlights the emotional complexities and unspoken narratives that often define personal spaces and relationships.
mainsPoints: Discuss how contemporary Indian literature reflects and critiques societal norms and family structures, particularly in the context of women's experiences.
Analyze the portrayal of grief and loss in literature as a tool for social commentary.
Examine the cultural nuances of family dynamics in specific regions of India, such as Kerala, as depicted in literary works.
Evaluate the power of narrative in bringing to light 'invisible' social or emotional issues within private spheres.
GS1.imageDescription: An artistic depiction of an empty, traditional Kerala home, evoking a sense of loss and memory.
GS1.trivia: Kerala's traditional architecture, often characterized by 'Nalukettu' (four-block homes), reflects distinct cultural and social structures.

GS1.headline: Diverse New Books Hit the Shelves, Covering History, Culture, and Social Commentary
GS1.explainer:
• A fresh wave of books has been released, offering a wide array of genres from historical analyses to contemporary fiction.
• The collection includes works exploring cultural transformations, deep dives into historical literature, and critiques of modern societal trends.
• Readers can find everything from engaging literary historiography to poignant narratives about family and loss.
• This season's releases promise intellectual stimulation and diverse perspectives on India and the world.
GS1.imageDescription: A stack of diverse new books on a bookshelf, with varied titles and covers.
GS1.trivia: India is one of the world's largest publishers of books, with a significant output in English and various regional languages.

GS1.headline: Critique: Robert Galbraith's Latest Crime Thriller Fails to Impress, Dubbed 'Trek to Nowhere'
GS1.explainer:
• Robert Galbraith, also known as J.K. Rowling, has released a new crime thriller that has received a lukewarm critical reception.
• Reviewers have labeled the book a 'criminal waste of time and energy,' citing its excessive length and unengaging plot.
• The critique highlights issues with pacing, character development, and narrative structure, which detract from the reading experience.
• This assessment suggests that even established authors can sometimes miss the mark with new genre entries.
GS1.imageDescription: A blurred image of a book cover with a magnifying glass, symbolizing a crime thriller and critical review.
GS1.trivia: J.K. Rowling adopted the pseudonym Robert Galbraith to publish her crime novels featuring private detective Cormoran Strike, initially to publish without hype.

GS1.headline: New Murder Mystery Explores Themes of Wronged Women and Social Justice
GS1.explainer:
• A new murder mystery novel delves into a complex plot centered around wronged women and their pursuit of justice.
• The narrative combines elements of suspense and social commentary, highlighting systemic injustices faced by women.
• It explores themes of retribution, empowerment, and the often-overlooked struggles of marginalized individuals.
• This book is part of a growing trend in literature that uses popular genres to address serious social issues.
mainsPoints: Discuss how contemporary crime fiction can serve as a powerful medium for social commentary on issues like gender inequality and justice for marginalized groups.
Analyze the evolving portrayal of women in Indian literature, from victims to agents of change.
Examine the role of popular genres in raising awareness about complex social issues and influencing public perception.
Evaluate the effectiveness of narrative in highlighting systemic injustices and advocating for social reforms.
GS1.imageDescription: A silhouette of a woman standing in front of a mysterious, dark backdrop, symbolizing justice and mystery.
GS1.trivia: Many historical and mythological narratives in India feature strong female characters seeking justice against societal wrongs, predating modern feminist literature.

GS2.headline: Parliament Passes National Urban Health Mission (NUHM) 2.0 Bill
GS2.explainer:
• The Parliament has successfully passed the National Urban Health Mission (NUHM) 2.0 Bill, aiming to strengthen urban healthcare infrastructure.
• Focuses on improving primary healthcare services, especially for vulnerable populations in urban areas.
• Introduces provisions for digital health records, telemedicine, and community health workers in urban settings.
• The mission seeks to address the growing health disparities and infrastructure gaps in India's rapidly urbanizing landscape.
prelimsPoints: National Urban Health Mission (NUHM) is a sub-mission of the National Health Mission (NHM).
NHM comprises two sub-missions: National Rural Health Mission (NRHM) and NUHM.
Key objectives include reducing out-of-pocket health expenditure and improving access to quality healthcare.
mainsPoints: Analyze the challenges faced by urban healthcare systems in India and how NUHM 2.0 seeks to address them.
Discuss the role of digital health and telemedicine in achieving universal health coverage in urban areas.
Evaluate the effectiveness of community health worker models in improving urban health outcomes.
Examine the integration of NUHM 2.0 with other urban development schemes and its potential impact on smart cities.
GS2.imageDescription: A graphic depicting urban healthcare workers interacting with residents, with digital health symbols.
GS2.trivia: India's urban population is projected to reach 600 million by 2030, necessitating robust urban health infrastructure.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Status Quo on Repo Rate Amidst Global Economic Uncertainties
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) has decided to keep the repo rate unchanged at its latest meeting.
• The decision comes amidst persistent global economic uncertainties and a cautious approach to domestic inflation management.
• The RBI reiterated its commitment to supporting economic growth while ensuring price stability.
• Analysts suggest the move reflects a wait-and-watch approach as global central banks navigate inflationary pressures and potential recession risks.
prelimsPoints: Repo Rate: Rate at which commercial banks borrow money from the RBI.
Monetary Policy Committee (MPC): Statutory body responsible for fixing the benchmark interest rate.
Primary objective of monetary policy in India: maintaining price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Quantitative and qualitative tools of monetary policy.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors influencing the RBI's decision to maintain the status quo on the repo rate.
Discuss the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control with economic growth objectives.
Examine the impact of global economic uncertainties on India's monetary policy decisions.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current monetary policy tools in managing India's economic landscape.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic depicting an interest rate graph with a steady line, overlaid with RBI logo.
GS3.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) was constituted in 2016 and consists of six members – three officials of the RBI and three external experts nominated by the Government of India.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Tests Next-Gen Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Prototype
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) has achieved a significant milestone with the successful test flight of its next-generation Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) prototype.
• The test involved a controlled landing experiment, simulating the return of a spacecraft to Earth.
• This technology is crucial for reducing the cost of space launches and enhancing India's competitive edge in the global space market.
• The RLV program aims to develop fully reusable orbital launch vehicles, similar to space shuttle technology.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in Science & Technology
prelimsPoints: RLV-TD (Reusable Launch Vehicle – Technology Demonstrator) is a precursor to a two-stage-to-orbit (TSTO) fully reusable launch vehicle.
Objectives of RLV program: reduce launch costs, increase launch frequency.
Other countries/agencies developing RLV technology (e.g., SpaceX, Blue Origin).
Different stages of ISRO's RLV development.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of Reusable Launch Vehicle technology for India's space program and its economic implications.
Analyze the challenges and technological advancements required for developing fully reusable orbital launch vehicles.
Evaluate how RLV technology can boost India's position in the global commercial space launch market.
Examine the potential applications of RLV beyond satellite launches, such as space tourism or in-orbit servicing.
GS3.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of ISRO's RLV prototype descending towards a landing strip.
GS3.trivia: ISRO first successfully tested the RLV-TD in 2016, demonstrating its hypersonic flight and re-entry capabilities.

GS3.headline: Government Unveils New 'Blue Economy' Policy Framework for Sustainable Ocean Resources
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Government has announced a comprehensive 'Blue Economy' policy framework aimed at sustainable utilization of India's ocean resources.
• The policy focuses on marine fisheries, aquaculture, ocean energy, marine biotechnology, and coastal tourism.
• Emphasizes job creation, environmental conservation, and enhancing India's maritime security and trade.
• It seeks to integrate various ocean-related sectors to unlock the economic potential of India's vast coastline and Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ).
prelimsPoints: Concept of 'Blue Economy' and its pillars.
India's coastline length and Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ).
Key initiatives under the 'Deep Ocean Mission'.
Importance of oceans for India's economy and security.
mainsPoints: Discuss the potential of India's 'Blue Economy' and the challenges in its sustainable development.
Analyze how the new policy framework can contribute to India's economic growth and environmental conservation goals.
Examine the role of technology and international cooperation in harnessing ocean resources sustainably.
Evaluate the interlinkages between the Blue Economy, coastal community livelihoods, and maritime security.
GS3.imageDescription: An infographic showing various components of the Blue Economy like shipping, fisheries, and renewable energy.
GS3.trivia: India's coastline is over 7,500 km long, with an Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) of over 2 million sq km, rich in living and non-living resources.

GS3.headline: Himalayan States Collaborate on Sustainable Tourism Framework to Protect Fragile Ecosystems
GS3.explainer:
• Several Indian Himalayan states have joined forces to develop a common framework for sustainable tourism.
• The initiative aims to regulate tourist activities, promote eco-friendly practices, and protect the fragile Himalayan ecosystems from over-tourism.
• Focuses on community-based tourism, waste management, and responsible infrastructure development.
• This collaborative effort seeks to balance economic benefits from tourism with the imperative of environmental conservation in the sensitive mountain regions.
prelimsPoints: Key Himalayan states in India and their ecological significance.
Impact of tourism on fragile mountain ecosystems.
Concept of 'carrying capacity' in tourism.
Examples of eco-tourism initiatives in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges faced by the Himalayan states due to over-tourism and climate change.
Analyze how a collaborative sustainable tourism framework can mitigate environmental degradation and promote responsible tourism.
Examine the role of local communities in developing and managing sustainable tourism practices in mountain regions.
Evaluate the balance between economic development through tourism and the need for ecological preservation in sensitive areas.
GS3.imageDescription: A serene landscape of the Himalayas with a small, eco-friendly homestay, symbolizing sustainable tourism.
GS3.trivia: The Himalayas are home to a vast array of biodiversity, including many endemic species, and are often referred to as the 'Water Tower of Asia' due to their massive ice and snow reserves.

GS3.headline: India's Q3 FY25 GDP Growth Forecast Revised Upwards by Leading Agencies
GS3.explainer:
• Several prominent economic agencies have revised India's GDP growth forecast for the third quarter of Fiscal Year 2025 upwards.
• The optimistic outlook is attributed to strong domestic demand, robust manufacturing output, and a resurgence in private investment.
• This positive revision signals resilience in the Indian economy amidst a challenging global economic environment.
• The government's continued focus on capital expenditure and structural reforms is seen as a key driver for sustained growth momentum.
prelimsPoints: Definition of GDP and its components (consumption, investment, government spending, net exports).
Methods of calculating GDP (expenditure method, income method, production method).
Role of different sectors (agriculture, industry, services) in India's GDP.
Major economic indicators tracked by government and agencies.
mainsPoints: Discuss the key drivers contributing to India's upward revised GDP growth forecast for Q3 FY25.
Analyze the significance of strong domestic demand and private investment for sustainable economic growth in India.
Examine the challenges that could potentially hinder India's long-term growth trajectory despite short-term positives.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the government's fiscal policies and structural reforms in boosting economic resilience.
GS3.imageDescription: An upward trending graph with Indian Rupee symbols and a growth arrow.
GS3.trivia: India is projected to be one of the fastest-growing major economies globally in the coming years, driven by its large domestic market and demographic dividend.

sources: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
